prior_version: v1 (corrected for SynchronizedRef usage in orchestrator)
mission: Deep-dive into campaign state machine correctness

state_machine_analysis.architecture: Two-tier declarative state machine with a pure deterministic reducer (reduceCampaignState)

LANE_STATE_MACHINE.note: The 'failed' state is NOT defined in LANE_STATE_MACHINE spec. It's handled via a manual switch case in secretary.ts lines 408-409: 'Failed' events set newState = 'failed' bypassing the declarative SM.

blockedTransitions.detail: Spread into every non-terminal campaign state. 3 transitions with priority escalation: conflict (pri 0) > child blocked (pri 1) > scope unsafe (pri 2). Only state WITHOUT blockedTransitions is 'blocked' itself.

reduceCampaignState_function.signature: reduceCampaignState(previous: CampaignState, events: readonly RuntimeEvent[], spec: AgentStateMachineSpec): CampaignState

algorithm.early_return: If events is empty, return previous unmodified
algorithm.budget_copy: Copy retryBudgets as working mutable record
algorithm.event_loop: For each event, get current state's spec, sort transitions by priority (HIGHEST first, descending), evaluate each predicate in order
algorithm.first_match_wins: On first matching predicate: record stateHistory, set currentState, increment transitionCount, execute entryActions
algorithm.return: Return new CampaignState snapshot with mutated budget values

reduceCampaignState_function.determinism: Pure function — same inputs always produce same output. EntryActions mutate retryBudgets in-place within the pass, but this is deterministic given the event order.

predicate_evaluation.note: Heuristic evaluation per code comment at line 8-9. No default case in switch — unknown predicates return false.

concurrency_analysis.overall_summary: Two different concurrency models: secretary uses plain Ref (atomic reads and writes with Effect runtime serialization), orchestrator uses SynchronizedRef (atomic read-modify-write with internal mutex). The most interesting race window is in the orchestrator's processEvent, but it's mitigated by SynchronizedRef's lock.

secretary_concurrency_model.summary: Uses plain Ref<Map<string, LaneState>>. Effect's Ref provides atomic read/write for individual operations.
secretary_concurrency_model.immutable_pattern: Every Ref.update creates const next = new Map(map) then mutates the copy.
secretary_concurrency_model.no_explicit_locks: Zero Semaphore, Mutex, Lock, or Fiber.join in secretary.ts

risk.rationale: Effect's Ref provides sequential atomicity per its runtime. Ref.get + Ref.update are separate operations but Effect serializes fiber execution at yield points. A processEvent that runs Ref.get then later Ref.update on the same lane won't race in practice because all event processing for a given lane happens sequentially within the same fiber. There is no fan-out for a single lane's events.

orchestrator_concurrency_model.summary: Uses SynchronizedRef<Map<string, OrchestratorCampaignState>> (line 218). This is Effect's concurrent-safe reference with internal mutex for atomic read-modify-write.
key_helpers: getState (line 220): SynchronizedRef.get(store) → map.get(campaignId) — atomic snapshot read
updateState (line 229): SynchronizedRef.update(store, (map) => { clone → fn(existing) → return }) — atomic RMW by lock

subtle_race.description: reduceCampaignState(state.smState, [event], ...) is computed against the snapshot from getState (line 354). Then updateState (line 356) passes fn = (s) => ({...s, smState: nextSM}) to SynchronizedRef.update. Inside the lock, fn(existing) receives the latest existing state for the campaign — but nextSM was computed from the stale snapshot, not from the existing.smState. If another fiber modified the campaign SM state between lines 354 and the lock acquisition at line 356, the reducer output nextSM doesn't account for that modification.
subtle_race.scenario: Fiber A: getState → sees state X → compute nextSM_A = reduce(X.smState, event_A). Fiber B: getState → sees state X → compute nextSM_B = reduce(X.smState, event_B). Fiber B: updateState (acquires lock first) → writes nextSM_B. Fiber A: updateState (acquires lock after B) → writes nextSM_A. → nextSM_A was computed from stale X.smState, not from nextSM_B. Effect: event_B's transition is LOST.
subtle_race.mitigation: Unlikely in practice because the orchestrator typically processes events in a single sequential fiber. The dispatchLanes path (line 370) calls processEventInternal which has the same race.
subtle_race.remediation: Move reduceCampaignState inside the SynchronizedRef.update callback so it computes against existing.smState under the lock: SynchronizedRef.update(store, (map) => { const existing = map.get(id); return new Map(map).set(id, {...existing, smState: reduce(existing.smState, ...)}); })

processEvent_race_analysis.note_on_synchronizedref_strength: SynchronizedRef is strictly stronger than plain Ref for this use case. The update callback runs under an internal mutex, so the MAP-level access is serialized. Only the computation-before-lock pattern is a concern.

orchestrator_concurrency_model.cross_campaign_isolation: Key-level independent. Two processEvent calls on different campaign IDs don't conflict at the Map level. But SynchronizedRef serializes ALL updates to the same Map, so a slow update for campaign A would block campaign B's update from acquiring the lock.

handler_cleanup_analysis.event_handlers: No event handler registration/unregistration pattern. Push-driven: events → EventStore → reduceCampaignState. No addEventListener/removeEventListener/Subject/Observable.

events_array.impact: Memory growth + O(n) predicate scans on allEvents for many predicate kinds (e.g., latest_validation_passed filters all events).
events_array.fix: Add event pruning for terminal states or rolling window.

projector_cache.issue: getProjection cache (Ref<Map<string, CampaignStateProjection>>) never evicts old entries.
projector_cache.impact: Unbounded Map growth as campaigns accumulate.
projector_cache.fix: Add LRU eviction or TTL expiry.

binder_map.issue: BinderService uses Ref<Map<string, Binder>> without eviction.
binder_map.impact: Would eventually grow large with many completed campaigns.

slow_transition_analysis.reduceCampaignState_complexity: O(events × transitions_per_state). Each new event iterates sorted transitions (typically 2-5 per state), and each predicate may scan all events.

lane_recovery_cost.issue: createLane replays ALL stored events through reduceCampaignState in one batch. For 10,000 events × 100 lanes = 1M predicate evaluations on recovery.
lane_recovery_cost.fix: Store periodic state snapshots for incremental replay.

event_store_i/o_ordering.issue: EventStore write (line 382) BEFORE reduceCampaignState (line 388). If write succeeds but state update fails, event is persisted but SM not advanced. On recovery, the event is replayed and the SM catches up.
event_store_i/o_ordering.note: This is safe for eventual consistency (compensating recovery) but means EventStore writes happen even for events that don't transition the SM.

cross_campaign_impact.rationale: A slow transition in campaign A does NOT block campaign B's processing in secretary (Ref is key-independent). However in orchestrator, SynchronizedRef serializes ALL updates to the store Map, so a slow updateState for campaign A WOULD delay campaign B's access to the lock.

risk_findings.title: Orchestrator processEvent has read-modify-write gap
risk_findings.description: processEvent computes reduceCampaignState against a SynchronizedRef.get snapshot (line 354), then passes the result via closure to SynchronizedRef.update (lines 356-358). The reducer output is based on stale state if another fiber modified the campaign SM state between the get and the lock acquisition. In the worst case, this loses an event's state transition entirely.
risk_findings.remediation: Move reduceCampaignState inside the SynchronizedRef.update callback so it computes against the latest existing.smState under the mutex. Severity is LOW because the orchestrator typically processes events in a single fiber.

risk_findings.title: Event array grows unbounded in CampaignState
risk_findings.description: reduceCampaignState concatenates allEvents = [...previous.events, ...events] without pruning. For long-running campaigns, this consumes increasing memory and makes O(n) predicate evaluations slower over time.
risk_findings.remediation: Add event pruning for terminal states. Consider a rolling window for predicates that only need recent events.

risk_findings.title: Projector cache never evicts
risk_findings.description: CampaignProjector caches projections in an unbounded Map in a Ref. No LRU, TTL, or size limit. Memory leak for systems that create many campaigns.
risk_findings.remediation: Add TTL-based eviction or LRU cache wrapper.

risk_findings.title: Lane recovery replays all events through synchronous state machine
risk_findings.description: createLane replays ALL stored events through reduceCampaignState synchronously. For campaigns with many events, blocks the Effect fiber.
risk_findings.remediation: Store periodic state snapshots so recovery only replays events since the last snapshot.

risk_findings.title: Failed state not defined in LANE_STATE_MACHINE spec
risk_findings.description: LANE_STATE_MACHINE defines 'blocked' and 'returned' as terminal states but NOT 'failed'. secretary.ts processEvent handles 'Failed' events via a manual switch case that bypasses the declarative state machine. The 'failed' state has no formal transitions, predicates, or entry actions in the spec.
risk_findings.remediation: Add 'failed' as a terminal state in LANE_STATE_MACHINE, or move Failed event handling into the SM spec.

risk_findings.title: EventStore write before SM transition creates partial-failure window
risk_findings.description: processEvent writes to EventStore (line 382) before running reduceCampaignState (line 388). If EventStore write succeeds but Ref.update fails, the event is stored but the in-memory SM state is not advanced. On recovery, the event is replayed and the SM catches up. Safe for eventual consistency but surprising on first read.
risk_findings.remediation: Document as intentional. The compensating recovery pattern is safe.

risk_findings.title: No test coverage for campaign module
risk_findings.description: Zero test files exist for the campaign module. No tests cover reduceCampaignState, evaluatePredicate, transition logic, secretary processEvent, or orchestrator lifecycle.
risk_findings.remediation: Add unit tests for reduceCampaignState, evaluatePredicate, and integration tests for secretary/orchestrator.

risk_findings.title: Predicate evaluation is heuristic per code comment
risk_findings.description: Code comment: 'Predicate evaluation is heuristic (SM-003 will make it rigorous with a proper predicate engine)'. evaluatePredicate uses simple event-matching, not a formal predicate engine.
risk_findings.remediation: Track SM-003 milestone. No immediate action.

risk_findings.title: SynchronizedRef serialization across campaigns
risk_findings.description: All campaign state updates in the orchestrator serialize through a single SynchronizedRef<Map<>>. A slow updateState call for one campaign delays all other campaigns' access to the lock.
risk_findings.remediation: Consider sharding: one SynchronizedRef per campaign ID, or use separate Refs for independent campaigns.

design_strengths: Pure deterministic reducer (reduceCampaignState) makes the state machine testable and replayable
Immutable copy pattern for all Ref/SynchronizedRef updates prevents shared-mutation bugs
Declarative spec cleanly separates state definitions, transitions, predicates, and entry actions
Two-tier machine (LANE + CAMPAIGN) keeps independent lane lifecycle separate from campaign coordination
Orchestrator uses SynchronizedRef (not plain Ref) — stronger concurrency guarantee with internal mutex
Blocking transitions spread across all campaign states via blockedTransitions() with priority escalation
critical_concerns: Event array and memory caches grow without bound across 3 subsystems (severity MEDIUM, 3 findings)
Orchestrator's processEvent has a subtle race window (severity LOW, mitigated by SynchronizedRef and single-fiber runtime)
Failed state bypasses the declarative SM spec (severity LOW)
Zero tests for the entire campaign module (severity INFO but practically the highest risk for regression safety)
summary.overall_assessment: The campaign state machine is well-architected with a clean pure-reducer design and appropriate use of Effect's synchronization primitives (SynchronizedRef for the orchestrator, plain Ref for the secretary). The most impactful finding is SM-001 (orchestrator processEvent race), but its practical severity is LOW due to SynchronizedRef's internal locking and the single-fiber runtime model. The most pressing concern is the unbounded memory growth (SM-002, SM-003) which will become a problem for long-running campaigns with many events, and SM-009 (Single-point serialization) if many campaigns run concurrently. The lack of tests (SM-007) means any refactoring of this subsystem carries high regression risk.
